data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse resulted from an endogenous legitimacy crisis in warrior-chariot aristocracies who dismantled redistributive obligations after prolonged military stalemate","mechanism": "Palatial economies depended on aristocratic loyalty maintained through gift-exchange and campaign plunder; when the Hittite-Mitanni-Egyptian equilibrium eliminated large-scale territorial expansion (c. 1250 BC), aristocratic retinues redirected coercive capacity toward internal predation, stripping palaces of labor and surplus through feudalization-from-above","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Levantine city-states (as independent actors)", "Sea Peoples as causal agents"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Linear B archives show palace labor forces and textile production at Pylos increasing, not contracting, immediately before destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite royal inscriptions show no aristocratic revolt or usurpation in final decades; succession proceeds normally", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom also experienced military stalemate post-Qadesh yet shows no analogous aristocratic predation in papyri", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Highly atypical: rejects both climatic and invasion frameworks in favor of internal class-conflict mechanism. Emphasizes political economy of aristocratic military organization rather than material scarcity."}
